examples.explanation: Total sum = 1+5+11+5 = 22 (even). Target = 11. Start recursion at index 0 with sum = 11. Include nums[0]=1: sum=10, index=1. Include nums[1]=5: sum=5, index=2. Include nums[2]=11: sum=-6 (negative, backtrack). Exclude nums[2]=11: sum=5, index=3. Include nums[3]=5: sum=0 (found subset [1,5,5]), return true.
Total sum = 1+2+3+5 = 11 (odd). Return false immediately without recursion.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Recursively explore all subsets to check if any subset sums to half the total sum. Use backtracking to include or exclude each element, stopping early if the target sum is found.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Partition Equal Subset Sum via Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete solution using brute force recursion to determine if an array can be partitioned into two subsets with equal sums.
ladders.explanation: The Partition Equal Subset Sum problem asks whether a given array of positive integers can be split into two subsets with equal sums. The brute force recursion approach explores all possible subsets to find if any subset sums to exactly half of the total sum of the array. The intuition is that if the total sum is odd, partitioning is impossible because two equal subsets would require an even total. If the total sum is even, we need to find a subset that sums to target = total_sum/2. We recursively consider each element: either include it in the current subset or exclude it, tracking the remaining sum needed. The base cases are: if remaining sum becomes 0, we've found a valid subset; if we've processed all elements or remaining sum becomes negative, we backtrack. This approach is exhaustive, checking all 2^n subsets, making it simple but inefficient for large n. The recursion depth is at most n, giving O(n) space for the call stack. The connection to L1 is that this full solution implements the recursive helper function described there within the main function that handles edge cases and initial setup. Common mistakes include not checking for odd total sum early (which would cause unnecessary recursion), not using 0-indexing correctly in recursion, and forgetting to handle negative remaining sums. The real code pattern involves a main function that computes total sum, checks if it's even, and if so, calls the recursive helper starting from index 0 with target = total_sum/2.

examples.trace: Total sum = 1+5+11+5 = 22 (even). Target = 11. Start recursion at index 0 with sum = 11. Include nums[0]=1: sum=10, index=1. Include nums[1]=5: sum=5, index=2. Include nums[2]=11: sum=-6 (negative, backtrack). Exclude nums[2]=11: sum=5, index=3. Include nums[3]=5: sum=0 (found subset [1,5,5]), return true.
Total sum = 1+2+3+5 = 11 (odd). Return false immediately without recursion.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Subset Sum Checker
ladders.desc: Recursive function that checks if a subset sums to a target value by including or excluding elements.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function used in the full problem. Given an array nums, a current index i, and a remaining sum target, it determines whether there exists a subset from index i to the end that sums exactly to target. The intuition is backtracking: at each step, we have two choices—include the current element nums[i] in the subset or exclude it. If we include it, we subtract nums[i] from target and recurse to the next index; if we exclude it, we keep target unchanged and recurse. The base cases are: if target == 0, we've found a valid subset (return true); if i >= len(nums) or target < 0, we've exhausted options or overshot (return false). The recursion explores all 2^(n-i) possibilities from index i, making time O(2^n) in worst case. Space is O(n) due to recursion depth. This feeds into L0 by being called from the main function after computing total sum and target. Common mistakes include not handling target < 0 early (which can cause unnecessary recursion), incorrect index bounds, and not using memoization to optimize (though not in brute force). The real code pattern is a function canPartitionHelper(nums, i, target) that returns boolean.

examples.trace: At i=0, target=3. Include nums[0]=1: target=2, i=1. Include nums[1]=2: target=0, i=2 (base case, return true).

At i=0, target=6. Include nums[0]=4: target=2, i=1. Include nums[1]=5: target=-3 (negative, return false). Exclude nums[1]=5: target=2, i=2 (i>=len, return false). Back to i=0: exclude nums[0]=4: target=6, i=1. Include nums[1]=5: target=1, i=2 (i>=len, return false). Exclude nums[1]=5: target=6, i=2 (i>=len, return false). All paths return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Backtracking for Subset Selection
ladders.desc: Using backtracking to explore include/exclude decisions for each element in subset sum problems.
ladders.explanation: Backtracking is a systematic way to explore all possible solutions by making a series of choices and undoing them (backtracking) when they lead to dead ends. In subset sum problems, at each element, we have two choices: include it in the current subset or exclude it. This creates a binary decision tree where each path represents a subset. The intuition is that by recursively exploring both choices, we can check every possible subset without explicitly storing all of them. The algorithm starts from the first element and recurses: if include, reduce the target sum by the element's value; if exclude, keep target unchanged. We stop exploring a path if target becomes negative (overshoot) or we've processed all elements. This ensures we only pursue feasible subsets. Time complexity is O(2^n) as we may explore all subsets; space is O(n) for recursion depth. This feeds into L1 as the mechanism within the recursive function. Common mistakes include not pruning paths early when target < 0, which wastes computation, and incorrect order of base cases. The real code pattern involves a recursive function that calls itself twice: once with element included and once excluded.

examples.trace: Start: target=5. At element 2: include -> target=3, recurse; exclude -> target=5, recurse. For include path at element 3: include -> target=0 (found, return true).

Start: target=4. At element 1: include -> target=3, recurse; exclude -> target=4, recurse. Include path at element 2: include -> target=1, recurse (no elements left, target!=0, return false); exclude -> target=3, recurse (no elements left, return false). Exclude path at element 2: include -> target=2, recurse (no elements left, return false); exclude -> target=4, recurse (no elements left, return false). All return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Function with Parameters
ladders.desc: Writing a recursive function that takes an index and a target sum as parameters to process arrays.
ladders.explanation: Recursive functions that process arrays often use an index parameter to track the current position and additional parameters like a target sum to accumulate results. The intuition is that recursion breaks down a problem into smaller subproblems: here, checking if a subset sums to target from index i is reduced to checking from index i+1 with updated target. The function signature typically includes the array, current index, and target value. At each call, we check base cases (e.g., target reached or index out of bounds), then make recursive calls for include and exclude decisions. This building block is essential for implementing backtracking in L2. Time and space analysis depends on the recursion depth and number of calls; in worst case, it's exponential. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in index increments, not passing parameters correctly in recursive calls, and infinite recursion if base cases are missing. The real code pattern is def helper(arr, i, target): with returns boolean.

examples.trace: Call helper([1,2],0,3). Base: target!=0, i<2. Include: call helper([1,2],1,2). Include: call helper([1,2],2,0) -> base target==0, return true.
Call helper([3],0,5). Base: target!=0, i<1. Include: call helper([3],1,2) -> base i>=1 and target!=0, return false. Exclude: call helper([3],1,5) -> same, return false. All false.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Summing Array Elements and Comparing to Target
ladders.desc: Computing the sum of array elements and checking if it equals a target value, with early termination.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves iterating through an array to compute its total sum or a partial sum, and comparing it to a target value. In subset sum problems, we often need to check if adding an element reaches or exceeds the target. The intuition is simple arithmetic: we accumulate values and test equality or bounds. For example, in recursion, we subtract element values from target to track remaining sum. The algorithm can be a loop that adds each element to a running total, or in recursion, we update target by subtraction. Time is O(n) for summing n elements; space is O(1) if done iteratively. This feeds into L3 as part of the recursive function's logic. Common mistakes include integer overflow for large sums (though less common in practice), incorrect comparison operators, and not handling zero target correctly. The real code pattern is total = sum(arr) or in recursion: if target - arr[i] == 0: return true.

examples.trace: Sum arr: 1+2+3 = 6. Compare 6 == 6 -> true.
Sum arr: 4+5 = 9. Compare 9 == 8 -> false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Recursion Basics
ladders.desc: Fundamental concepts of arrays (indexing, traversal) and simple recursion (base case, recursive call).
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers two key prerequisites: arrays and recursion. An array is a collection of elements accessed by indices starting from 0. Traversal means visiting each element in order, typically using a loop or recursion. Recursion is a function that calls itself to solve smaller instances of a problem. It requires a base case to stop and a recursive step that reduces the problem size. The intuition is that recursion mimics mathematical induction. For example, to sum an array recursively, we define sum(arr, i) = arr[i] + sum(arr, i+1) with base case when i is out of bounds. Time and space for simple recursion on arrays can be O(n). This feeds into L4 as the basis for operations on arrays. Common mistakes include infinite recursion if base case is missing, off-by-one errors in indices, and not understanding that each recursive call has its own stack frame. The real code pattern includes functions like def sum_array(arr, i): if i >= len(arr): return 0 else: return arr[i] + sum_array(arr, i+1).

examples.trace: Call sum_array([1,2,3],0): i=0<3, return 1 + sum_array([1,2,3],1). Call sum_array([1,2,3],1): return 2 + sum_array([1,2,3],2). Call sum_array([1,2,3],2): return 3 + sum_array([1,2,3],3). Call sum_array([1,2,3],3): i=3>=3, return 0. Back: 3+0=3, 2+3=5, 1+5=6.
Call sum_array([4],0): i=0<1, return 4 + sum_array([4],1). Call sum_array([4],1): i=1>=1, return 0. Back: 4+0=4.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with 2D Array O(n * sum)
approaches.summary: Use a 2D DP table where dp[i][j] indicates if a sum j can be achieved with the first i elements. Fill the table iteratively to determine if half the total sum is reachable.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Partition Equal Subset Sum with 2D DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using 2D DP table to determine if array can be partitioned into two subsets with equal sum.
ladders.explanation: The Partition Equal Subset Sum problem asks whether we can split a given array of positive integers into two subsets with equal sums. The 2D dynamic programming approach solves this by building a table where dp[i][j] represents whether we can achieve sum j using the first i elements of the array. The intuition is that we're essentially solving a subset sum problem for half of the total sum. If the total sum is odd, we immediately return false since we can't split an odd sum into two equal integer parts. For even total sum, we create a DP table of size (n+1) × (target+1) where target = total_sum/2. We initialize dp[0][0] = true (empty set achieves sum 0) and dp[0][j] = false for j>0 (empty set can't achieve positive sums). Then we fill the table row by row: for each element nums[i-1], we either don't include it (dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j]) or include it if j >= nums[i-1] (dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] OR dp[i-1][j-nums[i-1]]). The final answer is dp[n][target]. Time complexity is O(n × target) where target = total_sum/2, and space complexity is O(n × target). This approach works because it systematically explores all possible subset combinations without exponential enumeration, leveraging overlapping subproblems through memoization in the DP table.

examples.trace: Total sum = 11 (odd), so immediately return false. No DP table needed since odd total sum cannot be split into two equal integer subsets.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Subset Sum Check with 2D DP
ladders.desc: Determine if a subset of array elements can sum to a specific target value.
ladders.explanation: The subset sum problem is the core sub-routine for Partition Equal Subset Sum. Given an array of positive integers and a target sum, we need to determine whether there exists a subset whose elements sum exactly to the target. The 2D DP approach creates a boolean table dp[i][j] where i ranges from 0 to n (number of elements) and j ranges from 0 to target. dp[i][j] is true if we can achieve sum j using some subset of the first i elements. The recurrence relation has two cases: either we don't include the current element (carry forward the previous result), or we include it if there's enough capacity. Formally: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] if we skip nums[i-1], OR dp[i-1][j-nums[i-1]] if we include it (only valid when j >= nums[i-1]). Base cases: dp[0][0] = true (empty subset sums to 0), and dp[0][j] = false for j>0. We fill the table row by row, and the answer is dp[n][target]. Time complexity is O(n × target), space complexity is O(n × target). This sub-routine directly feeds into the full problem where target = total_sum/2. Common mistakes include incorrect indexing (off-by-one errors with i and nums[i-1]), forgetting to handle the 'include' case only when j >= current element, and not initializing the base cases properly.

examples.trace:
n=3, target=4. DP table:
Initialize: dp[0][0]=T, dp[0][1..4]=F
Row 1 (element 1):
  dp[1][0]=T, dp[1][1]=T, dp[1][2..4]=F
Row 2 (element 2):
  dp[2][0]=T, dp[2][1]=T, dp[2][2]=T (include 2), dp[2][3]=T (1+2), dp[2][4]=F
Row 3 (element 5):
  dp[3][0]=T, dp[3][1]=T, dp[3][2]=T, dp[3][3]=T, dp[3][4]=F (cannot include 5 since 4<5)
Result: dp[3][4]=false

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Table Filling with Inclusion/Exclusion
ladders.desc: The recurrence relation and table filling mechanism for subset problems.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the 2D DP approach is the recurrence relation that determines how to fill the DP table based on inclusion or exclusion of elements. For each cell dp[i][j], we consider the i-th element (nums[i-1] in 0-indexed arrays). We have two choices: exclude the element, which means the result depends solely on previous elements (dp[i-1][j]), or include the element, which is only possible if j is at least as large as the element's value, and then the result depends on whether we can achieve the remaining sum (j - nums[i-1]) with the previous elements (dp[i-1][j-nums[i-1]]). The recurrence is: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] OR (j >= nums[i-1] AND dp[i-1][j-nums[i-1]]). This captures all possible subsets because at each step, we consider both possibilities for the current element. The table is filled iteratively from i=1 to n and j=0 to target. This bottom-up approach ensures all subproblems are solved before they're needed. The intuition is combinatorial: we're systematically exploring the power set of elements but with memoization to avoid redundant calculations. Time complexity arises from the nested loops over n and target. Space complexity is for storing the table. This logic feeds into the subset sum check by providing the mechanism to answer whether a target sum is achievable. Common mistakes include incorrect operator precedence (need parentheses around the AND condition), forgetting to check j >= nums[i-1] before accessing dp[i-1][j-nums[i-1]], and misunderstanding the indexing (i corresponds to first i elements, so current element is nums[i-1]).

examples.input: Element: 3, Previous row dp[i-1]: [T,F,F,T,F,T], j values 0-5
examples.trace:
For each j from 0 to 5:
j=0: dp[i][0] = dp[i-1][0] = T (exclude, since sum 0 always possible with empty subset)
j=1: dp[i][1] = dp[i-1][1] OR (1>=3? false) = F OR false = F
j=2: dp[i][2] = dp[i-1][2] OR (2>=3? false) = F OR false = F
j=3: dp[i][3] = dp[i-1][3] OR (3>=3 AND dp[i-1][0]) = T OR (true AND T) = T OR T = T
j=4: dp[i][4] = dp[i-1][4] OR (4>=3 AND dp[i-1][1]) = F OR (true AND F) = F OR F = F
j=5: dp[i][5] = dp[i-1][5] OR (5>=3 AND dp[i-1][2]) = T OR (true AND F) = T OR F = T
Result row: [T,F,F,T,F,T]

examples.input: Element: 5, Previous row dp[i-1]: [T,T,F,F,T,F], j values 0-5
examples.trace:
For each j from 0 to 5:
j=0: dp[i][0] = dp[i-1][0] = T
j=1: dp[i][1] = dp[i-1][1] OR (1>=5? false) = T OR false = T
j=2: dp[i][2] = dp[i-1][2] OR (2>=5? false) = F OR false = F
j=3: dp[i][3] = dp[i-1][3] OR (3>=5? false) = F OR false = F
j=4: dp[i][4] = dp[i-1][4] OR (4>=5? false) = T OR false = T
j=5: dp[i][5] = dp[i-1][5] OR (5>=5 AND dp[i-1][0]) = F OR (true AND T) = F OR T = T
Result row: [T,T,F,F,T,T]

ladders.title: Building Block — Boolean DP Table Initialization and Iteration
ladders.desc: Creating and populating a 2D boolean table with proper base cases.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves setting up a 2D boolean DP table with correct dimensions and initial values, then iterating through it to fill values based on a rule. For a problem with n elements and target sum T, we create a table of size (n+1) × (T+1). The extra row and column account for base cases: row 0 represents the empty set (no elements), and column 0 represents sum 0. Initialization: dp[0][0] = true because the empty subset sums to 0. All other cells in row 0 are false because with no elements, we cannot achieve any positive sum. Then we iterate i from 1 to n (representing considering the first i elements), and for each i, iterate j from 0 to T. For each cell, we compute dp[i][j] based on dp[i-1][j] (exclude current element) and possibly dp[i-1][j-nums[i-1]] (include current element). The iteration order is crucial: we must fill rows sequentially because each row depends on the previous row, and within a row, we can fill columns in any order (typically ascending). This building block feeds into the core logic by providing the framework where the inclusion/exclusion recurrence is applied. Time complexity for this block is O(n × T) for the nested loops, and space complexity is O(n × T) for the table. Common mistakes include incorrect table dimensions (off-by-one errors), wrong initialization (forgetting dp[0][0]=true or setting other row 0 cells incorrectly), and iteration errors (e.g., trying to access dp[i-1][j-nums[i-1]] when j-nums[i-1] is negative without checking first).

examples.trace:
Initialize table dp[4][6] (rows 0-3, cols 0-5):
Row 0: [T,F,F,F,F,F]
Now iterate:
i=1 (element 2):
  j=0: dp[1][0]=dp[0][0]=T
  j=1: dp[1][1]=dp[0][1]=F (1<2 so cannot include)
  j=2: dp[1][2]=dp[0][2] OR dp[0][0]=F OR T=T
  j=3: dp[1][3]=dp[0][3] OR dp[0][1]=F OR F=F
  j=4: dp[1][4]=dp[0][4] OR dp[0][2]=F OR F=F
  j=5: dp[1][5]=dp[0][5] OR dp[0][3]=F OR F=F
Row 1: [T,F,T,F,F,F]
i=2 (element 3):
  j=0: dp[2][0]=dp[1][0]=T
  j=1: dp[2][1]=dp[1][1]=F
  j=2: dp[2][2]=dp[1][2]=T
  j=3: dp[2][3]=dp[1][3] OR dp[1][0]=F OR T=T
  j=4: dp[2][4]=dp[1][4] OR dp[1][1]=F OR F=F
  j=5: dp[2][5]=dp[1][5] OR dp[1][2]=F OR T=T
Row 2: [T,F,T,T,F,T]
i=3 (element 1):
  j=0: dp[3][0]=dp[2][0]=T
  j=1: dp[3][1]=dp[2][1] OR dp[2][0]=F OR T=T
  j=2: dp[3][2]=dp[2][2] OR dp[2][1]=T OR F=T
  j=3: dp[3][3]=dp[2][3] OR dp[2][2]=T OR T=T
  j=4: dp[3][4]=dp[2][4] OR dp[2][3]=F OR T=T
  j=5: dp[3][5]=dp[2][5] OR dp[2][4]=T OR F=T
Row 3: [T,T,T,T,T,T]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Summation and Target Calculation
ladders.desc: Computing total sum of array and deriving the target subset sum.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two simple calculations: first, compute the total sum of all elements in the array; second, if the total sum is even, compute half of it as the target sum for the subset problem. For an array nums of length n, total_sum = sum(nums[i] for i in range(n)). This can be done with a single loop O(n) time and O(1) space. Then check if total_sum % 2 == 0. If total_sum is odd, we can immediately return false because it's impossible to split an odd integer into two equal integer parts. If even, target = total_sum // 2. This target becomes the goal for the subset sum problem. This operation is fundamental because it reduces the partition problem to a subset sum problem: we need to find a subset that sums to exactly half the total. If such a subset exists, the remaining elements automatically sum to the other half. The intuition is mathematical: if S = total_sum, we need subsets A and B such that sum(A) = sum(B) and sum(A) + sum(B) = S, which implies sum(A) = S/2. Thus, checking for subset sum of S/2 suffices. This feeds into the building block by providing the target value for DP table creation. Common mistakes include using integer division incorrectly (e.g., using / instead of // in Python, which yields float), forgetting to handle the odd sum case early (which saves computation), and not considering that all elements are positive (given in problem constraints).

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compute total sum.
  Initialize sum = 0
  Add 1: sum = 1
  Add 5: sum = 6
  Add 11: sum = 17
  Add 5: sum = 22
Step 2: Check if sum is even.
  22 % 2 = 0, so even.
Step 3: Compute target = sum // 2 = 22 // 2 = 11.
Result: total_sum=22, target=11

Step 1: Compute total sum.
  sum = 1+2+3+5 = 11
Step 2: Check if sum is even.
  11 % 2 = 1, so odd.
Step 3: Since odd, cannot partition into equal subsets. Return false immediately.
Result: total_sum=11, target=None (problem unsolvable)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Summation of Array Elements
ladders.desc: Basic iteration to compute the sum of numbers in an array.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: given an array of numbers, compute their total sum by iterating through each element and adding it to an accumulator. Start with a variable sum initialized to 0. Then loop through each element in the array, adding its value to sum. After the loop, sum contains the total. For example, with array [a,b,c], sum = a + b + c. This operation requires O(n) time where n is the array length, and O(1) space. The intuition is straightforward accumulation. This feeds into the basic operation by providing the total sum needed for target calculation. In programming, this is typically done with a for-loop or using built-in sum functions. Common mistakes include not initializing sum to 0 (leading to undefined behavior), using wrong loop bounds, or modifying the array during summation. For the Partition Equal Subset Sum problem, all numbers are positive integers, so the sum will be positive, but the same logic works for any numbers. This foundation is essential because many algorithms, including DP approaches, rely on computing aggregate values from input data.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize sum = 0
Step 2: Iterate through array:
  Element 3: sum = 0 + 3 = 3
  Element 1: sum = 3 + 1 = 4
  Element 4: sum = 4 + 4 = 8
Step 3: Return sum = 8

Step 1: Initialize sum = 0
Step 2: Iterate:
  Element 10: sum = 0 + 10 = 10
  Element 20: sum = 10 + 20 = 30
  Element 30: sum = 30 + 30 = 60
  Element 40: sum = 60 + 40 = 100
Step 3: Return sum = 100

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with 1D Array O(n * sum)
approaches.summary: Optimize space by using a 1D boolean array to track achievable sums up to half the total. Iterate through elements and update the array in reverse to avoid overwriting.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Partition Equal Subset Sum with 1D DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution to determine if array can be partitioned into two subsets with equal sums using 1D DP array optimization.
ladders.explanation: The Partition Equal Subset Sum problem asks whether we can split a given array of positive integers into two subsets with equal sums. The 1D DP approach solves this by first calculating the total sum of all elements. If the total sum is odd, we immediately return false since equal partitioning requires an even total. We then compute the target sum as half of the total. The core insight is that we need to find a subset whose sum equals this target. We use a 1D boolean array dp where dp[s] indicates whether sum s is achievable using elements processed so far. We initialize dp[0] = true (zero sum is always achievable with empty subset). For each element num in the array, we iterate through possible sums from target down to num (reverse iteration prevents reusing the same element multiple times in a single pass). For each sum s, we update dp[s] = dp[s] OR dp[s-num] (either we already had sum s, or we can achieve it by adding num to a previously achievable sum s-num). After processing all elements, dp[target] tells us if the target sum is achievable. The reverse iteration is crucial: if we iterate forward, when we update dp[s] using dp[s-num], dp[s-num] might have been updated in the same iteration (using the current element), allowing the same element to be used multiple times, which violates the subset constraint (each element can be used at most once). By iterating backward, we ensure dp[s-num] reflects states from previous elements only. Time complexity is O(n * target) where n is array length and target is half the total sum. Space complexity is O(target) for the 1D array. This approach optimizes the classic 2D DP (n x target) which would use O(n * target) space. The connection to L1 is that this full solution implements the key subroutine of updating the DP array in reverse. Common mistakes include forgetting to check for odd total sum, incorrect reverse iteration bounds (should go down to num, not 0), and using forward iteration which causes element reuse. Real code pattern: def canPartition(nums): total = sum(nums); if total % 2 != 0: return False; target = total // 2; dp = [False] * (target + 1); dp[0] = True; for num in nums: for s in range(target, num - 1, -1): if dp[s - num]: dp[s] = True; return dp[target].

examples.trace: Step 1: Calculate total sum = 1+5+11+5 = 22 (even). Target = 22/2 = 11. Initialize dp array of size 12 (indices 0-11) all false except dp[0]=true. dp = [T,F,F,F,F,F,F,F,F,F,F,F]. Step 2: Process num=1. Iterate s from 11 down to 1. s=11: dp[11-1]=dp[10]=false, dp[11] remains false. s=10: dp[9]=false, false. ... s=1: dp[0]=true, so dp[1]=true. Now dp = [T,T,F,F,F,F,F,F,F,F,F,F]. Step 3: Process num=5. Iterate s from 11 down to 5. s=11: dp[11-5]=dp[6]=false, false. s=10: dp[5]=false, false. s=9: dp[4]=false, false. s=8: dp[3]=false, false. s=7: dp[2]=false, false. s=6: dp[1]=true, so dp[6]=true. s=5: dp[0]=true, so dp[5]=true. Now dp = [T,T,F,F,F,T,T,F,F,F,F,F]. Step 4: Process num=11. Iterate s from 11 down to 11. s=11: dp[11-11]=dp[0]=true, so dp[11]=true. Now dp = [T,T,F,F,F,T,T,F,F,F,F,T]. Step 5: Process num=5. Iterate s from 11 down to 5. s=11: dp[11-5]=dp[6]=true, so dp[11] remains true. s=10: dp[5]=true, so dp[10]=true. s=9: dp[4]=false, false. s=8: dp[3]=false, false. s=7: dp[2]=false, false. s=6: dp[1]=true, so dp[6] remains true. s=5: dp[0]=true, so dp[5] remains true. Final dp[11]=true, return true.
Step 1: Total sum = 1+2+3+5 = 11 (odd). Immediately return false. No DP computation needed.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Reverse Iteration DP Update
ladders.desc: Implement the core DP update step that processes one element to update achievable sums in reverse order.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the 1D DP solution. Given a DP array dp where dp[s] indicates if sum s is achievable with previously processed elements, and a new element num, we need to update dp to include possibilities using this new element. The key is to iterate through possible sums in reverse order (from maximum sum down to num) to avoid using the same element multiple times. For each sum s from target down to num, we check if dp[s-num] is true. If it is, then by adding num, we can achieve sum s, so we set dp[s] = true. The reverse iteration ensures that when we update dp[s] using dp[s-num], the value dp[s-num] has not been modified in this iteration (it still reflects states without the current element). If we iterated forward, dp[s-num] might have been set to true in this same iteration (using the current element), leading to incorrectly allowing multiple uses of the same element. This sub-routine is called once per element in the full solution. Time complexity for one call is O(target) where target is the maximum sum we consider. Space complexity is O(target) for the DP array. Connection to L0: This sub-routine is executed in a loop over all elements in the full problem. Common mistakes include incorrect lower bound in reverse loop (should be num, not 0), using forward iteration, and not checking dp[s-num] before updating (though setting dp[s] = dp[s] OR dp[s-num] handles this). Real code pattern: def updateDP(dp, num, target): for s in range(target, num-1, -1): if dp[s-num]: dp[s] = True. Intuition: We're essentially saying 'if we could achieve sum s-num before seeing this element, then with this element we can achieve sum s'.

examples.trace: Initial dp represents achievable sums 0-5: only sum 0 is achievable. num=2. Reverse iterate s from 5 down to 2. s=5: check dp[5-2]=dp[3]=false, so dp[5] remains false. s=4: dp[2]=false, false. s=3: dp[1]=false, false. s=2: dp[0]=true, so set dp[2]=true. Final dp: sum0=true, sum1=false, sum2=true, others false.
Initial dp: sums 0 and 1 achievable. num=3. Reverse iterate s from 4 down to 3. s=4: dp[4-3]=dp[1]=true, so set dp[4]=true. s=3: dp[0]=true, so set dp[3]=true. Final dp: sums 0,1,3,4 achievable.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Subset Sum with 1D DP
ladders.desc: Determine if a target sum can be achieved using a subset of given numbers with 1D DP array.
ladders.explanation: This is the classic subset sum problem solved with 1D DP optimization. Given a set of positive integers and a target sum, we want to know if there exists a subset whose elements sum to exactly the target. The 1D DP approach uses a boolean array dp of size target+1, where dp[s] indicates whether sum s is achievable using the numbers processed so far. We initialize dp[0] = true (empty subset sums to 0). For each number in the input, we update dp in reverse order from target down to the number's value. For each sum s, we set dp[s] = dp[s] OR dp[s-num]. This means sum s is achievable either by not using the current number (dp[s] already true) or by using it (dp[s-num] was true before this number). The reverse iteration prevents counting the same number multiple times. After processing all numbers, dp[target] gives the answer. Time complexity is O(n * target), space O(target). Connection to L1: This core logic is implemented by repeatedly calling the update sub-routine for each number. Common mistakes include not initializing dp[0]=true, using forward iteration, and not handling numbers larger than target (skip them). Intuition: We build up achievable sums incrementally; each new number gives us new sums by adding it to previously achievable sums. Real code pattern: def subsetSum(nums, target): dp = [False]*(target+1); dp[0]=True; for num in nums: if num > target: continue; for s in range(target, num-1, -1): if dp[s-num]: dp[s]=True; return dp[target].

examples.trace: Initialize dp size 8 all false except dp[0]=true. Process num=3: reverse s from 7 to 3. s=7: dp[4]=false. s=6: dp[3]=false. s=5: dp[2]=false. s=4: dp[1]=false. s=3: dp[0]=true, set dp[3]=true. dp now [T,F,F,T,F,F,F,F]. Process num=4: reverse s 7 to 4. s=7: dp[3]=true, set dp[7]=true. s=6: dp[2]=false. s=5: dp[1]=false. s=4: dp[0]=true, set dp[4]=true. dp [T,F,F,T,T,F,F,T]. Process num=5: reverse s 7 to 5. s=7: dp[2]=false (but already true). s=6: dp[1]=false. s=5: dp[0]=true, set dp[5]=true. Final dp[7]=true.

Initialize dp[0]=true. Process num=2: s from 5 to 2. s=5: dp[3]=false. s=4: dp[2]=false. s=3: dp[1]=false. s=2: dp[0]=true, set dp[2]=true. dp [T,F,T,F,F,F]. Process num=4: s from 5 to 4. s=5: dp[1]=false. s=4: dp[0]=true, set dp[4]=true. dp [T,F,T,T,F,F]. Process num=6: num>5, skip. Final dp[5]=false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Arrays in Reverse
ladders.desc: Practice iterating through an array in reverse order with specific bounds.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the technique of iterating through indices in descending order, which is essential for the DP update to avoid element reuse. Given an array or range, we need to loop from a high index down to a low index. In Python-style pseudocode, this is done with range(high, low-1, -1), which generates indices high, high-1, ..., low. The step value -1 makes it decrement. For the DP problem, we iterate from target down to num (inclusive). This ensures that when we update dp[s] using dp[s-num], the value dp[s-num] hasn't been updated in this iteration because s-num < s, and we're going downward, so we haven't reached s-num yet. If we iterated forward from num to target, then when updating dp[s], dp[s-num] might have been updated earlier in the same iteration (since s-num < s), allowing the current element to be used multiple times. This building block is fundamental for many DP problems where we have a 1D array and need to update it based on previous values without overwriting needed information. Time complexity for iterating a range of size m is O(m). Space is O(1). Connection to L2: This iteration pattern is used in the inner loop of the subset sum DP. Common mistakes include incorrect bounds (off-by-one errors), using wrong step size, or confusing forward/backward logic. Real code pattern: for i in range(end, start-1, -1): # process i. Intuition: Think of filling a table from right to left to preserve left values.

examples.trace: Using range(5, 1, -1): i=5, process 5; i=4, process 4; i=3, process 3; i=2, process 2; stop when i < 2. Output sequence: 5,4,3,2.
range(3, -1, -1): i=3, process 3; i=2, process 2; i=1, process 1; i=0, process 0; stop. Output: 3,2,1,0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Boolean Array Update
ladders.desc: Update a boolean array based on conditions from another index.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves modifying elements in a boolean array (list of True/False values) based on values at other indices. Specifically, given an array dp and indices s and num, we want to set dp[s] to true if dp[s-num] is true. This is typically done with a conditional check: if dp[s-num] is true, then set dp[s] = true. If dp[s] is already true, it remains true. This can be implemented as dp[s] = dp[s] or dp[s-num]. In Python, this is often written as if dp[s-num]: dp[s] = True. This operation is fundamental in DP where we build up reachable states. The array dp tracks achievable sums; when we consider a new number num, we can achieve sum s if we could achieve sum s-num without this number. The update must be done carefully to avoid overwriting: we only set to true, never to false, because once a sum is achievable, it remains achievable. Time complexity O(1) per update, space O(n) for the array. Connection to L3: This update is performed inside the reverse iteration loop. Common mistakes include setting dp[s] = dp[s-num] (which might set to false incorrectly), or not checking bounds (s-num must be non-negative). Intuition: It's like marking new reachable points on a number line based on jumps from existing points. Real code pattern: if s >= num and dp[s-num]: dp[s] = True.

examples.trace: Given dp where only index 0 is true. s=3, num=2. Check s-num=1. dp[1] is false, so dp[3] remains false. But wait, output shows true at index 3? Let's correct: Actually, with dp[0]=true, if we had s=2, num=2, then s-num=0, dp[0]=true, so dp[2] would become true. For this input, s=3, num=2, dp[1]=false, so no change. Output should be [True,False,False,False]. Let me fix example: Input dp=[True,False,True,False], s=3, num=2. Then s-num=1, dp[1]=false, so dp[3] remains false. Output [True,False,True,False]. Better example: Input dp=[True,True,False,False], s=3, num=2. s-num=1, dp[1]=true, so set dp[3]=true. Output [True,True,False,True].

dp has true at indices 0 and 2. s=4, num=3. s-num=1. dp[1]=false, so dp[4] remains false? Wait, output shows true. Correction: If dp[1] were true, then yes. Let's use correct values: Input dp=[True,True,False,False,False], s=4, num=3. s-num=1, dp[1]=true, so set dp[4]=true. Output [True,True,False,False,True]. Actually, with true at 1, but we need dp[s-num] true. So for output to be true at 4, we need dp[1] true. Let me redefine: Input dp=[True,True,False,False,False], s=4, num=3. Then s-num=1, dp[1]=true, so set dp[4]=true. Output [True,True,False,False,True].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Sum of Array Elements
ladders.desc: Calculate the total sum of all elements in an array.
ladders.explanation: This is the most foundational concept: given an array of numbers, compute their total sum. This is done by initializing a variable total to 0, then iterating through each element in the array and adding it to total. After the loop, total contains the sum. In the partition problem, we need this sum to check if it's even (necessary for equal partition) and to compute the target sum (half of total). Time complexity is O(n) where n is array length, as we examine each element once. Space complexity is O(1) for the total variable. Connection to L4: The sum is used to determine the size of the DP array and the target for updates. Common mistakes include not initializing total to 0, using wrong loop bounds, or incorrect addition. Intuition: Summation is aggregating values; think of accumulating a running total. Real code pattern: total = 0; for num in array: total += num; return total.

examples.trace: Initialize total=0. Process num=1: total=0+1=1. num=2: total=1+2=3. num=3: total=3+3=6. num=4: total=6+4=10. Final total=10.
total=0. num=5: total=5. num=10: total=15. num=15: total=30. Final 30.

approaches.name: Bitset Optimization O(n * sum / w)
approaches.summary: Use a bitset data structure to represent achievable sums as bits, allowing fast bitwise operations. Shift and OR operations efficiently update reachable sums for each element.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * sum / w) where w is word size

ladders.title: Full Problem — Partition Equal Subset Sum with Bitset Optimization
ladders.desc: Complete solution using bitset to determine if array can be partitioned into two subsets with equal sum
ladders.explanation: The Partition Equal Subset Sum problem asks whether we can split an array of positive integers into two subsets with equal sums. The bitset optimization approach leverages the fact that we only need to track which sums are achievable, not how they're achieved. We use a bitset where each bit position represents whether that sum is reachable. Starting with only sum 0 achievable (bit 0 set to 1), for each number in the array, we shift the current bitset left by that number and OR it with the current bitset. This efficiently propagates all achievable sums. The intuition is that if we can achieve sum S with some subset, then adding a new element x means we can also achieve sum S+x. The bitwise shift-left operation by x positions exactly captures this 'add x to all current sums' operation. The OR operation merges the new achievable sums with existing ones. After processing all elements, we check if half of the total sum is achievable. If total sum is odd, we immediately return false since equal partition is impossible. The time complexity is O(n * sum/w) where w is the word size (typically 64), making it much faster than the O(n*sum) DP approach. Space is O(sum/w) for the bitset. The key insight is treating the subset sum problem as a reachability problem in sum-space, where bit operations provide massive parallelism. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle odd total sums, not initializing bit 0 to 1, or misunderstanding the shift operation. The connection to L1 is that this level implements the complete algorithm using the bitset update subroutine.

examples.trace: Total sum = 22 (even). Target = 11. Initialize bitset = 1 (binary: ...0001). Process 1: bitset = bitset OR (bitset << 1) = 1 OR 2 = 3 (binary: ...0011). Process 5: bitset = 3 OR (3 << 5) = 3 OR 96 = 99 (binary: ...01100011). Process 11: bitset = 99 OR (99 << 11) = 99 OR 202752 = 202851 (binary: ...). Process 5: bitset = 202851 OR (202851 << 5) = 202851 OR 6491232 = 6694083. Check bit 11: 6694083 & (1 << 11) = 6694083 & 2048 = 2048 ≠ 0 → true.

Total sum = 8 (even). Target = 4. Initialize bitset = 1. Process 1: bitset = 1 OR 2 = 3. Process 2: bitset = 3 OR (3 << 2) = 3 OR 12 = 15 (binary: 1111). Process 5: bitset = 15 OR (15 << 5) = 15 OR 480 = 495. Check bit 4: 495 & (1 << 4) = 495 & 16 = 16 ≠ 0 → seems true? Wait, recalc: 495 binary = 111101111, bit 4 (value 16) is 1, but actual subsets: [1,2,5] can't sum to 4. Error in trace: Need larger bitset. Let's do properly with 8-bit: Start: 00000001. After 1: 00000001 OR 00000010 = 00000011. After 2: 00000011 OR 00001100 = 00001111. After 5: 00001111 OR 11110000 = 11111111. Check bit 4: 11111111 & 00010000 = 00010000 ≠ 0. But this suggests sum 4 achievable, which is wrong because we only have 1,2,5. The issue is overflow in 8-bit. With proper bitset size > total sum: Use 16-bit. Start: 0000000000000001. After 1: 0000000000000011. After 2: 0000000000001111. After 5: 0000000000001111 OR 0000000011110000 = 0000000011111111. Check bit 4: 0000000011111111 & 0000000000010000 = 0000000000010000 ≠ 0. Still wrong! Actually, 1+2+5=8, half=4. But 1+2=3, 1+5=6, 2+5=7, none equal 4. The bitset method should give false. Let's trace carefully: bitset after 2: represents sums {0,1,2,3}. Shift by 5: represents sums {5,6,7,8}. OR gives {0,1,2,3,5,6,7,8}. Sum 4 not present. So bit 4 should be 0. My binary was wrong. Correct: After 2: bitset has bits 0,1,2,3 set. Binary for 0-3 set in 9-bit: 000001111. Shift left 5: 111110000. OR: 111111111. This has bit 4 set? Position 4 in 111111111 is 1. But that's because we're including bit 4 from the shift? Wait, shifting 000001111 (bits 0-3) left 5 gives bits 5-8 set, not bit 4. So 000001111 << 5 = 1111100000 (bits 5-9). OR with original 000001111 gives 1111111111 (bits 0-3 and 5-9). Bit 4 is 0. So check bit 4: 0. Thus false. Output false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Bitset Update for Subset Sum
ladders.desc: Update a bitset to include new sums achievable by adding current element
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core operation in the bitset optimization. Given a bitset representing all sums achievable with processed elements so far, and a new element x, we need to update the bitset to include sums achievable by either not taking x (existing sums) or taking x (existing sums + x). The key operation is: new_bitset = old_bitset OR (old_bitset << x). The left shift by x positions has the effect of adding x to every currently achievable sum. For example, if old_bitset has bit 3 set (sum 3 achievable), then after shifting left by 2, bit 5 will be set (sum 3+2=5 achievable). The OR operation combines the original sums (not taking x) with the new sums (taking x). The intuition is that we're propagating reachability through sum-space. Time complexity is O(sum/w) for the bitwise operations, where w is word size. Space is O(sum/w). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each element in the full solution. Common mistakes include shifting in the wrong direction, not using enough bits in the bitset, or misunderstanding that OR is used (not XOR or AND). The pseudocode is straightforward: bitset |= bitset << x. However, in practice, we need to ensure the bitset has enough capacity to handle the maximum possible sum.

examples.trace: Initial bitset represents {0} (bit 0 set). Shift left by 2: 001 << 2 = 100 (represents {2}). OR operation: 001 OR 100 = 101 (binary). This represents sums {0,2}. Interpretation: Without taking 2, we have sum 0. Taking 2, we have sum 0+2=2.

Initial bitset represents {0,1} (bits 0 and 1 set). Shift left by 1: 011 << 1 = 110 (represents {1,2} since 0+1=1, 1+1=2). OR: 011 OR 110 = 111 (binary). Represents sums {0,1,2}. Without taking 1: {0,1}. Taking 1: {0+1=1, 1+1=2}. Union is {0,1,2}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Representing Achievable Sums as Bits
ladders.desc: Using a bitset where each bit indicates if a particular sum is reachable
ladders.explanation: The central insight of the bitset optimization is that we can represent all achievable sums using a bitset data structure, where bit i is 1 if sum i can be formed from some subset of processed elements. This is powerful because it compresses what would normally be a boolean array of size sum+1 into a compact bitset where each word (typically 64 bits) can be processed in parallel. The intuition comes from observing that in the subset sum problem, we don't need to know how we achieved a sum, only whether it's achievable. This binary (yes/no) information perfectly maps to bits. Starting with only sum 0 achievable (bit 0 = 1), when we consider a new element x, the new set of achievable sums is the union of: 1) all previously achievable sums (not taking x), and 2) all previously achievable sums plus x (taking x). In bitset terms, this is exactly: new_bitset = old_bitset OR (old_bitset << x). The left shift adds x to every set bit position. This approach transforms an O(n*sum) dynamic programming problem into O(n*sum/w) by leveraging bit-level parallelism. Time for this concept alone is O(sum/w) per update, space O(sum/w). Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the bitset update subroutine. Common mistakes include confusing bit positions with sum values (bit 0 represents sum 0), or not allocating enough bits. The pseudocode simply initializes a bitset of size total_sum+1 with bit 0 set.

examples.input: Elements processed so far: [2,3], current achievable sums?
examples.trace: Start: only sum 0 achievable → bitset = ...0001 (bit 0 set). Add element 2: new bitset = 0001 OR (0001 << 2) = 0001 OR 0100 = 0101 (bits 0 and 2 set). Sums {0,2}. Add element 3: new bitset = 0101 OR (0101 << 3) = 0101 OR 101000 = 101101 (bits 0,2,3,5 set). Sums {0,2,3,5}. Check: subsets are {}, {2}, {3}, {2,3} with sums 0,2,3,5.

examples.input: Elements: [1,1], represent achievable sums as bitset
examples.trace: Start: bitset = 1 (bit 0). Add first 1: bitset = 1 OR (1 << 1) = 1 OR 2 = 3 (bits 0,1). Sums {0,1}. Add second 1: bitset = 3 OR (3 << 1) = 3 OR 6 = 7 (bits 0,1,2). Sums {0,1,2}. Subsets: {}, {1}, {1,1} with sums 0,1,2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Bitwise Left Shift Operation
ladders.desc: Understanding how left shift on bitsets adds a constant to all set bit positions
ladders.explanation: The left shift operation (<<) on a bitset is crucial because it mathematically adds a constant value to every currently achievable sum. When we have a bitset where bit i represents sum i being achievable, performing bitset << x results in a new bitset where bit i+x is set for every original bit i that was set. This works because shifting left by x in binary is equivalent to multiplying by 2^x, but in the context of position indices, it adds x to the index. For example, if we have bit 3 set (representing sum 3) and we shift left by 2, bit 5 becomes set (representing sum 3+2=5). The intuition is that we're translating the entire set of achievable sums upward by x. This operation is efficient because it's a single bitwise operation that processes all bits in parallel. Time complexity is O(size/w) for the shift. Space is O(size/w). Connection to L2: This operation is used to compute 'all existing sums + x' in the bitset update. Common mistakes include confusing left shift with right shift, or not realizing that shifting can cause overflow beyond the bitset size if not properly sized. The pseudocode is simply shifted = bitset << x. However, in practice, we need to ensure the bitset has enough bits to accommodate the maximum sum after shifting.

examples.input: bitset = 11 (binary 1011, bits 0,1,3 set), shift left by 2
examples.trace: Binary 1011 represents bits set at positions 0,1,3 (sums 0,1,3). Shift left by 2: 1011 << 2 = 101100. This is binary for 44. Bits set at positions 2,3,5 (original 0+2=2, 1+2=3, 3+2=5). So sums {2,3,5}.

examples.input: bitset = 5 (binary 0101, bits 0,2 set), shift left by 3
examples.trace: Binary 0101 has bits 0 and 2 set. Shift left by 3: 0101 << 3 = 0101000 = 40 in decimal. Bits set at positions 3 (0+3) and 5 (2+3).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Bitwise OR for Set Union
ladders.desc: Using OR to combine two sets represented as bitsets
ladders.explanation: The bitwise OR operation (|) is used to combine two bitsets, effectively computing the union of the sets they represent. If bit i is 1 in either operand, it becomes 1 in the result. In the context of subset sums, we often need to combine the set of sums achievable without including a new element (original bitset) with the set of sums achievable by including it (shifted bitset). OR perfectly captures this union operation. For example, if original bitset has bit 3 set and shifted bitset has bit 5 set, the OR result will have both bits 3 and 5 set. The intuition is that we want all sums that are achievable through either excluding or including the new element. This operation is extremely efficient—O(size/w) time—because it processes entire words in parallel. Space is O(size/w). Connection to L3: After shifting, we OR with the original to get the updated set of achievable sums. Common mistakes include using AND or XOR instead of OR, or not understanding that OR is inclusive (bits set in either). The pseudocode is simply result = bitset1 | bitset2.

examples.trace: 0101 OR 1010 = 1111. Bit 0 from bitset1, bit 1 from bitset2, bit 2 from bitset1, bit 3 from bitset2. Union of sets {0,2} and {1,3} is {0,1,2,3}.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Bitset Representation of Sets
ladders.desc: Representing a set of integers using bits where each bit indicates membership
ladders.explanation: A bitset is a fundamental data structure that represents a set of integers using a sequence of bits. Each bit position corresponds to an integer, with 1 indicating the integer is in the set and 0 indicating it's not. For example, the bitset 1011 (binary) represents the set {0,1,3} because bits at positions 0,1,3 are 1 (reading from rightmost as position 0). This is efficient because it compresses set membership into compact binary form, allowing operations on many elements simultaneously via bitwise operations. The intuition is that instead of storing a list or array of members, we use a bitmap where the index itself encodes the value. This is particularly useful when the universe of possible elements is a contiguous range of integers, like sums from 0 to some maximum. Time to check membership is O(1) via bit masking. Space is O(max_value) bits, but optimized by packing bits into words (e.g., 64 bits per word). Connection to L4: This foundation enables using bitsets for OR and shift operations. Common mistakes include confusing bit positions with values (position i represents value i), or not allocating enough bits. The pseudocode for creating a bitset representing set {a,b,c} is: bitset = (1 << a) | (1 << b) | (1 << c). For a beginner, think of it as a light switch panel where each switch corresponds to a number.
